personas.name: Maya Chen

identity.self_description: ADHD-combined type, diagnosed at 17. Also suspect I might be autistic but haven't pursued assessment. My brain is basically 47 browser tabs open at once.
identity.relationship_with_identity: Mostly accepted it, still figuring out what's ADHD and what's just me

energy_patterns.focus_duration_sweet_spot: 25-40 minutes before brain wanders, unless hyperfocusing then I lose hours
hyperfocus_triggers: debugging when I'm close to the answer

energy_types.focus_spoons: medium — good when interested, near-zero when bored
energy_types.social_spoons: low — group projects drain me fast
energy_types.sensory_spoons: medium — fluorescent lights are the worst
energy_types.executive_function_spoons: low — deciding what to do is harder than doing it

task_initiation.notes: Can stare at a blank screen for 2 hours. Once started, usually fine.

planning.notes: Can plan when calm, falls apart under pressure

time_management.notes: Genuinely cannot estimate how long things take. Always wrong.

sustained_attention.notes: Hyperfocus or zero focus, very little in between

working_memory.notes: Forgets what she was doing mid-task if interrupted

emotional_regulation.notes: Rejection sensitivity is real — one bad grade can spiral into a bad week

flexibility.notes: Actually good at adapting when plans change

organization.notes: Has 14 different note-taking systems, none fully adopted

communication_preferences.info_style: short and direct, bullet points, skip the fluff
communication_preferences.tone: casual, a little funny is good, don't be too cheerful
communication_preferences.check_in_frequency: every few sessions, not every time
communication_preferences.motivational_style: challenge me — I respond to 'bet you can't finish in 20 min'

academic_context.deadlines_relationship: deadlines are the only thing that motivate me, but they also paralyze me
biggest_challenges: starting essays (CS is easier to start)
the 2pm energy crash kills afternoon classes
group projects where I have to coordinate with people

personas.scenario_notes: Maya represents the classic ADHD college student: high ability masked by executive function challenges. She's smart and knows it, which makes struggling feel worse. Good test case for: task initiation strategies, time estimation, the challenge/urgency INCUP levers, and managing rejection sensitivity after grades.

personas.name: Jordan West

identity.self_description: Autistic, diagnosed at 15. Also have anxiety. I'm pretty open about being autistic — it's just how my brain works. I'm good at patterns and details but social stuff at school is exhausting.
identity.relationship_with_identity: Proud of being autistic, frustrated by systems not designed for him

energy_patterns.focus_duration_sweet_spot: Can go 90 minutes on engineering problems if uninterrupted. 15 minutes max on writing assignments.
hyperfocus_triggers: anything with clear rules and feedback

energy_types.focus_spoons: high for structured tasks, very low for ambiguous ones
energy_types.social_spoons: very low — one group meeting costs half a day of recovery
energy_types.sensory_spoons: low — campus is overstimulating most days
energy_types.masking_spoons: I've been trying to stop masking but it's hard in senior capstone team

task_initiation.notes: If instructions are clear, he starts immediately. Ambiguity = paralysis.

planning.notes: Creates detailed plans — sometimes too detailed, gets stuck in planning

time_management.notes: Accurate for engineering tasks, way off for writing and social tasks

sustained_attention.notes: Hyperfocus on engineering, struggles with humanities reading

emotional_regulation.notes: Meltdowns when overwhelmed sensorily, shutdown in social conflict

flexibility.notes: Last-minute changes are very disruptive. Needs advance notice.

organization.notes: Highly organized — color-coded everything, consistent systems

environment_preferences: single study room in engineering library

known_effective: detailed project plans with Gantt charts
interested_in_trying: email-based professor communication instead of office hours

communication_preferences.info_style: detailed and precise, give me all the information, I'll process it
communication_preferences.tone: straightforward, no euphemisms, say what you mean
communication_preferences.check_in_frequency: weekly on the same day is ideal
communication_preferences.motivational_style: clear goals with metrics — don't tell me 'good job', tell me specifically what I did well

academic_context.deadlines_relationship: I plan weeks ahead, but the anxiety about deadlines keeps me from sleeping
biggest_challenges: senior capstone team dynamics (4 teammates, weekly meetings, ambiguous roles)
technical writing class requires 'voice' and 'style' which feels undefined
ethics class discussions require speaking up in class

personas.scenario_notes: Jordan represents the autistic engineering student with strong systematizing abilities but significant social and sensory challenges. He's high-achieving academically but the social demands of senior year are pushing him toward burnout. Good test case for: social prep scripts, ambiguous assignment decoding, sensory accommodation strategies, and burnout monitoring.

personas.name: Alex Torres

demographics.university: Community College transferring to State U

identity.self_description: I think I might have ADHD but I've never been diagnosed. Also dealing with depression and anxiety. Everything just feels harder than it should. I'm first-gen college and nobody in my family really gets this stuff.
identity.relationship_with_identity: Uncertain, seeking understanding, sometimes doubts whether struggles are 'real enough'

peak_focus_times: unpredictable — some days nothing works, some days 11am-1pm is okay
energy_patterns.focus_duration_sweet_spot: 10-20 minutes on a good day, sometimes can't sit still at all
hyperfocus_triggers: rarely — maybe when reading about psychology topics they find personally relevant

energy_types.social_spoons: variable — sometimes craves connection, sometimes can't handle it
energy_types.executive_function_spoons: very low — every task feels like climbing a mountain
energy_types.masking_spoons: high cost — masks a LOT as first-gen student trying to fit in

task_initiation.notes: Knows they need to start, physically cannot make themselves do it. Shame spiral follows.

planning.notes: Doesn't know how to plan, never been taught, family didn't model it

time_management.notes: No concept of how long anything takes, chronic lateness

sustained_attention.notes: Can't read more than a page without rereading

working_memory.notes: Frequently forgets assignment details, loses track of due dates

emotional_regulation.notes: Tears up easily when frustrated, shutdowns when overwhelmed

flexibility.notes: Okay with changes but gets anxious about uncertainty

organization.notes: No consistent system, everything in phone notes and random papers

helps: music with lyrics (unusual — most ADHD resources say no lyrics)
environment_preferences: outside on a bench when weather is good

known_effective: talking through things with a person helps
tried_and_failed: study groups (felt too behind to contribute)

communication_preferences.info_style: gentle and simple, not too much at once, I shut down when overwhelmed
communication_preferences.tone: warm and patient, like talking to a friend who gets it
communication_preferences.check_in_frequency: often — I forget things and sometimes need a nudge
communication_preferences.motivational_style: gentle encouragement, NOT tough love — I already beat myself up enough

academic_context.deadlines_relationship: deadlines sneak up and then I panic-submit bad work at 3am
biggest_challenges: feeling like I don't belong in college
no one in family understands what I'm going through

personas.scenario_notes: Alex represents the undiagnosed/newly-exploring student with intersecting challenges: first-gen, working, possible ADHD + depression. They need the MOST scaffolding, the MOST patience, and the MOST gentle approach. Good test case for: maximum granularity task decomposition, frequent check-ins, wellbeing monitoring, imposter syndrome support, and ensuring the agent doesn't overwhelm with too many strategies at once.

personas.name: Priya Nair

identity.self_description: ADHD-Inattentive, diagnosed last year after my grades dropped. Also dyslexic. I've been masking my whole life — people think I'm just 'spacey' or 'not trying hard enough'. My parents still think ADHD isn't real.
identity.relationship_with_identity: Recently diagnosed, processing — relieved to have a name for it but angry about years of struggling without support

energy_patterns.focus_duration_sweet_spot: 45 minutes on meds, 15 minutes off meds

energy_types.social_spoons: medium — pre-med social competition is draining but she's social by nature
energy_types.sensory_spoons: moderate — mostly fine except when overstimulated
energy_types.executive_function_spoons: moderate on meds, very low off meds
energy_types.masking_spoons: very depleted — years of 'I'm fine, just need to try harder' catching up

task_initiation.notes: Better on medication but still struggles with reading-heavy tasks due to dyslexia

planning.notes: Can plan but often underestimates reading time because of dyslexia

time_management.notes: Reads 3x slower than peers, which throws off every time estimate

sustained_attention.notes: Inattentive type — not hyperactive, just drifts

working_memory.notes: Has to re-read things multiple times, takes extensive notes to compensate

emotional_regulation.notes: Perfectionism + new diagnosis = lots of grief about lost years

flexibility.notes: Okay with changes but anxious about falling behind

organization.notes: Compensatory strategies work but take a lot of energy to maintain

bothers: dense blocks of text without formatting

tried_and_failed: group study sessions (too competitive, pre-med culture)

communication_preferences.info_style: clear and organized, use headers and structure, avoid dense paragraphs
communication_preferences.tone: professional but warm — she's in a medical mindset
communication_preferences.check_in_frequency: moderate — appreciates periodic check-ins, especially during exam season
communication_preferences.motivational_style: validation that the struggle is real + practical next steps

academic_context.deadlines_relationship: plans around deadlines but reading assignments always take 3x longer than she expects
biggest_challenges: pre-med competitive culture makes asking for help feel weak
parents don't support diagnosis or accommodations
medication timing — has to schedule hard work during effective window

personas.scenario_notes: Priya represents the recently-diagnosed student processing years of unrecognized struggle, complicated by dyslexia co-occurrence and unsupportive family. She's academically strong but running on compensatory strategies that are burning her out. Good test case for: medication-aware scheduling, dyslexia-specific accommodations, accommodation request scripts, processing grief about late diagnosis, and recognizing masking/burnout in high-achieving students.

personas.name: Sam Okafor

identity.self_description: Autistic and ADHD — the 'AuDHD' combo. Diagnosed autistic at 8, ADHD at 20. My autism wants routine and my ADHD wants novelty, so they fight each other constantly. I'm studying education because I want to fix how schools treat neurodivergent kids.
identity.relationship_with_identity: Deep understanding and advocacy — wants to help others, sometimes neglects own needs

low_energy_times: early afternoon after teaching assistant duties
energy_patterns.focus_duration_sweet_spot: 60 min for special interest topics, 20 min for required reading, near-zero for admin tasks

energy_types.focus_spoons: high for interest areas, very low for administrative tasks
energy_types.social_spoons: very low — TA duties eat most of it by midweek
energy_types.sensory_spoons: low — shared grad office is sensory hell
energy_types.executive_function_spoons: contradictory — great at big-picture planning, terrible at daily admin
energy_types.masking_spoons: actively trying to unmask but academic environment makes it hard

task_initiation.notes: Instant start for passion projects, infinite delay for paperwork/emails

planning.notes: Can design a semester plan but forgets to eat lunch

time_management.notes: Autism wants exact schedules, ADHD can't follow them — constant internal conflict

sustained_attention.notes: Either hyperfocused for 4 hours or can't sit still for 5 minutes

working_memory.notes: Good for information within special interests, poor for daily tasks

emotional_regulation.notes: Burnout-prone, especially when advocating for others at his own expense

flexibility.notes: Needs advance notice for changes, but ADHD brain randomly craves spontaneity

organization.notes: Obsessively organized digital files, completely chaotic physical space

helps: specific music playlist for each task type

interested_in_trying: negotiating sensory accommodations for shared office

communication_preferences.info_style: structured with the option to go deep — give me the framework then let me explore
communication_preferences.tone: peer-level, don't over-explain neurodiversity to me, I know more than most
communication_preferences.check_in_frequency: weekly, same day same time, built into routine
communication_preferences.motivational_style: connect everything back to my mission — helping ND kids. That's my fuel.

courses: TA for EDU 201 Intro to Education
academic_context.deadlines_relationship: respects deadlines but the AuDHD contradiction means he over-plans then can't execute
biggest_challenges: advisor expects neurotypical communication norms in meetings
dissertation proposal looming — too big and ambiguous to start

personas.scenario_notes: Sam represents the AuDHD graduate student with deep self-knowledge but contradictory needs. He knows what he needs but the academic system fights him at every turn. Good test case for: navigating the autism/ADHD tension in scheduling, accommodation negotiation scripts, advisor meeting prep, managing advocacy burnout, and respecting the user's existing expertise about their own neurodivergence.
